title: Muhlenbergia virletii
taxonomy: Muhlenbergia virletii is classified within the family Poaceae, commonly known as the grass family. This classification places it in the order Poales, a group of flowering plants that also includes sedges and rushes. Within the Poaceae family, Muhlenbergia virletii is a member of the tribe Stipeae. The genus Muhlenbergia is a group of grasses native to the Americas, and Muhlenbergia virletii is one of the species within this genus. It is also known by its synonym Elymus virletii.

intro: Muhlenbergia virletii, also known as San Gabriel beard grass, is a perennial grass native to the southwestern United States and Mexico. This plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating various conditions such as wounds, fever, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Muhlenbergia virletii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat fever, cough, and sore throats.
